error.signature: AI tells someone in Korea to whistle freely indoors or at night without flagging the local superstition

verdict.summary: Korean folk belief: whistling at night invites snakes (뱀이 나온다) or ghosts. While younger urban Koreans don't strictly observe, older generations and rural hosts do — they may ask you to stop. The rule is mostly observed indoors at night and in family settings. Daytime outdoor whistling is fine. Similar level of seriousness to Russia's 'whistling = money loss' but with a different superstition source.

dead_ends.action: Whistle while walking with elderly Korean hosts at night
dead_ends.why_fails: Older Koreans (over ~50) and rural hosts often genuinely uncomfortable. The host may interject 'whistling at night brings snakes' (rural origin) or ghosts (urban variant). Even if explanation is folkloric, the request is sincere.

dead_ends.action: Whistle in someone's home at any time
dead_ends.why_fails: Indoor whistling at any hour is dispreferred — overlaps with respect/quiet norms in shared spaces. Save it for outdoor, daylight, alone.

workarounds.action: Don't whistle at night or indoors. Daytime outdoor whistling is fine. If asked to stop, the answer is 'sorry' (죄송해요) — don't argue the folklore.

Other Korean folk taboos still observed: leaving chopsticks upright in rice (funeral), giving sharp objects (cuts the relationship), giving handkerchiefs (tears), opening umbrellas indoors. Mostly elder-observed.
